one_line_takeaway: Merely retiming existing spikes -- without changing any count or amplitude -- fools event-driven SNNs at over 90% success while touching under 2% of spikes on DVS-Gesture, exposing spike timing as a stealthy attack axis that current intensity/rate-based defenses and adversarial training cannot adequately counter.
key_finding: Timing-only, rate-preserving retiming is a highly effective and stealthy attack surface. On binary DVS grids, small per-spike jitter (B_inf, eps=1) already drives most models over 96% ASR. On DVS-Gesture the attack exceeds 90% ASR while touching fewer than 2% of spikes under B_0 (headline claim; B_0=4k touches 2.45% of spikes there). A consistent secondary finding is that integer grids are markedly more robust than binary grids under the same nominal B_1/B_0 budgets (multiplicity smooths pre-activations and stabilizes normalization), while B_inf saturates on both. A clear polarity pattern emerges: positive-channel spikes tend to be delayed and negative-channel spikes advanced. Adversarial training gives only partial robustness and collapses clean accuracy, and simple label-free filtering defenses (refractory/temporal/spatial smoothing) barely dent ASR without destroying clean accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Spike-Retiming Attacks, a timing-only adversarial threat against event-driven Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) that moves existing spikes along the time axis while strictly preserving spike counts and amplitudes (rate-preserving). Its own contributions are: (1) a formal capacity-1 spike-retiming threat model with a unified trio of budgets (per-spike jitter B_inf, total delay B_1, tamper count B_0) plus explicit feasibility constraints (spikes stay on the timeline, at most one spike per event-line/time-bin, value conservation); (2) Projected-in-the-Loop (PIL) optimization, which couples a differentiable soft retiming (shift-probability logits -> softmax over admissible target times) in the backward pass with a strict discrete projection P* in the forward pass, giving stable gradients without violating feasibility; (3) a budget-aware objective (PIL-PGD) that maximizes task loss and adds a capacity over-occupancy regularizer and a normalized-hinge budget penalty; and (4) a comprehensive empirical study across three event datasets, binary vs. integer grids, three budget types, adversarial training, and defenses. Note the Related-Work-cited attacks (RGA, HART, DVS-Attacks, SpikeFool, SCG, GSAttack, PDSG-SDA) are prior art the paper contrasts against, not its own method; the distinguishing claim is that all of those change intensities/counts, whereas this attack changes only timing.
threat_model: White-box, untargeted adversary (has model parameters). Capacity-1, rate-preserving timing-only perturbation: each existing spike (s,j) is assigned an integer shift delta so it replays at t = s+delta; no spike is created, deleted, or split, and at most one spike may occupy any pixel-polarity line / time-bin. Feasible retimings must stay on the timeline (0 <= s+delta < T). Attacker power is capped by one of three budgets over the active spike set A(x): B_inf (|delta_{s,j}| <= eps, local jitter), B_1 (sum |delta| <= eps, total timing shift), or B_0 (number of tampered spikes <= eps). Motivation given for realism: event/neuromorphic sensors naturally exhibit timestamp jitter and readout latency, so timing-only changes stay within sensor uncertainty, do not alter any frame-wise intensity or rate statistic, and thus evade standard intensity/rate-based detection. A targeted-attack variant (target label 0) is also evaluated but is weaker.
limitations: (1) Strictly white-box; transferability is only explored in the appendix and is architecture-dependent. (2) Targeted attacks are substantially weaker than untargeted (e.g., binary B_inf(1) drops to 26.8% ASR vs 96.4% untargeted on VGGSNN/DVS-Gesture), which the authors attribute to intrinsic difficulty of targeting event grids. (3) Effectiveness of the sparse/aggregate budgets degrades as time resolution grows: on the binary grid, going from T=20 to T=40 drops B_1(8k) ASR from 90.6% to 29.1% and B_0(4k) from 48.0% to 13.3%, because finer bins create more active positions to retime. (4) The proposed adversarial-training defense sacrifices heavy clean accuracy (binary AT clean accuracy 22-48%) and yields only modest robustness, so the paper offers no strong defense. (5) Stealth for B_0 is dataset-dependent: N-MNIST B_0(400) touches 14.2% of spikes, far from the sub-2% figure highlighted for DVS-Gesture. (6) Evaluated only on three event-vision datasets at T=10 default with white-box directly-trained SNNs.

datasets_used: CIFAR10-DVS (10,000 event streams, 10 classes, 2x128x128; 100 correctly-classified test samples attacked)
DVS-Gesture (1,063/288 train/test streams, 11 gestures, 2x128x128; all correctly-classified test samples attacked)
quantitative_results: DVS-Gesture: attack exceeds 90% ASR while touching <2% of spikes under B_0 (headline); B_0(4k) touches 2.45% of spikes
B_0 stealth footprints: DVS-Gesture 4k = 2.45% of spikes, CIFAR10-DVS 4k = 3.84%, N-MNIST 400 = 14.2%
Binary grid, B_inf(eps=1): most models over 96% ASR (near saturation)
Binary DVS-Gesture, SpikingResformer: 98.5% ASR at B_1(4k)
Binary CIFAR10-DVS, SpikingResformer: 96.0% ASR at B_0(4k)
Integer robustness gap: binary DVS-Gesture ResNet18 >99% ASR at B_1(8k) vs integer ~mid-60% at 8k, ~mid-80% at 16k
Clean accuracy examples (binary vs integer): ResNet18 DVS-Gesture 95.14 vs 94.40; CIFAR10-DVS 78.30 vs 79.20; N-MNIST 99.62 for both
Ablation (VGGSNN, DVS-Gesture), w/o PIL vs full: binary B_1(8k) 84.3 vs 98.5; integer B_inf(3) 63.0 vs 85.0
Ablation w/o R_p budget penalty: binary B_1 98.5 -> 76.6; integer B_1 79.1 -> 42.4
Time-bin sensitivity (binary): B_1(8k) ASR 90.6% at T=20 -> 29.1% at T=40; B_0(4k) 48.0% -> 13.3%
Targeted attack (VGGSNN/DVS-Gesture) much lower than untargeted: binary B_inf(1) 26.8%, B_1(8k) 57.3%, B_0(4k) 62.8%
Adversarial training (integer, B_1(8k) model): clean ~69%, robust acc >60% at small B_1, mid-50% at 8k; binary AT clean accuracy only 22.92-48.26%
baselines_compared: SpikeFool (Buchel et al., 2022) -- raw-event baseline, matched B_0 budgets (full tables in appendix)
PDSG-SDA (Lun et al., 2025) -- raw-event Sparse Dynamic Attack baseline, matched B_0 budgets; also used in filtering-defense and targeted comparisons
Internal ablations: w/o PIL (soft-only S_pi), w/o Cap (no capacity regularizer), w/o R_p (no budget penalty)
Neuron-model / config variants as internal comparisons: LIF (default) vs PLIF vs PSN; T=10/20/30/40; untargeted vs targeted
Filtering defenses: refractory, temporal, and spatial smoothing (label-free), on binary DVS-Gesture/VGGSNN
